name: Emeritus of Ideation // Ancestral Recall
cfbCommentary: .0 A massive flier with ward 2 and it gets to cast Ancestral Recall , maybe multiple times? I don't know what I need to sign away to open this one, but I'm in. I doubt the second Ancestral is going to be necessary, but it's nice to have it if it is.

name: Germination Practicum
cfbCommentary: .0 Five mana for a permanent +2/+2 boost can already be exciting for a creature-heavy deck. Now, what if I told you that you get it every turn for free, while the rest of your mana is available to keep adding bodies to the battlefield! Germination Practicum is an outrageous bomb, and most opponents won't last more than two turns or so once you cast it.

name: Moment of Reckoning
cfbCommentary: .0 // 4.0 So I've played this already, but not in a Silverquill deck. It was in a Prismari deck with Resonating Lute and Zaffai and the Tempests , which was actually awesome. I don't think most Silverquill decks are going to get to seven mana, so this is more of a wacky spell build-around for me.

name: Quandrix, the Proof
cfbCommentary: .0 A 6/6 flying trample (I refuse to call this flample, sorry) that also plays a random card for free? That's incredible, and giving your other spells cascade is just proof that this is the best. I'm always taking this if I'm either color, even on the splash, and it's a great reason to draft Quandrix.

name: Professor Dellian Fel
cfbCommentary: This is a return to the classic planeswalker template, but at four mana instead of five. That's a great place to be, and Professor Dellian Fel looks unbeatable if they don't have a huge board. It protects itself, draws cards, and gains life — there's not much more to ask.

name: Berta, Wise Extrapolator
cfbCommentary: .0 An unanswered Berta will usually be game over, as you can start growing it and making Fractals with ease. There are a lot of "kill this before they untap with it" creatures in this set, and it's good to be on the side of the creatures instead of hoping to have the right removal.

name: Jadzi, Steward of Fate // Oracle's Gift
cfbCommentary: Jadzi, Steward of Fate really has it all, and every deck will be happy to include it. A 2/4 for three that loots away cards twice would already be pretty strong, and this also gets you to spend five to nine mana to make a bunch of giant Fractuals (plus boosts your existing ones). That's a legit Limited all-star, and Jadzi makes me want to pick up bounce or Raise Dead -type effects to maximize how many times I get to do this.

name: Lorehold, the Historian
cfbCommentary: This is exactly the kind of Dragon you fear, and certainly justifies the mythic rarity. Lorehold, the Historian bashes for five immediately, lets you rummage on their upkeep, and can even Miracle some nice spells if it sticks around.

name: Emeritus of Truce // Swords to Plowshares
cfbCommentary: Emeritus of Truce is an excellent card. A 3/3 that comes with an extra flyer is great value that builds a lot of board presence early on. Don't try to overthink it and focus too much on the Swords to Plowshares part; you will want to use this card proactively most of the time. Of course, if you fall behind on board, which happens naturally in Limited (especially on the draw), being able to use this card defensively is an amazing bonus.

name: Conciliator's Duelist
cfbCommentary: This kicks things off with a free card, and the one-point ping is usually better for you, since Silverquill tends to be aggressive. It then lets you blink on demand, and can even blink itself (and it often will). The Duelist is a great reason to draft Silverquill, and one I'd be thrilled to open.

name: Silverquill, the Disputant
cfbCommentary: .0 You don't need much more than the stats/keywords on this, though sometimes you will sacrifice your worst creature to copy a spell. Take it early, and be happy to open a mythic Dragon.

name: Sylvan Library
cfbCommentary: Sylvan Library is green's second Special Guests card. While this is one of my favorite cards in powerful formats like Legacy, some readers might be surprised to see me give it a modest rating for Secrets of Strixhaven Limited. Getting the most out of Sylvan Library relies on one of two things. First, the ability to shuffle your library. (Stack the best card on top, draw it, and shuffle away the other two). Second, a willingness to trade four life for a card. In Limited, shuffling your library is

name: Emeritus of Abundance // Regrowth
cfbCommentary: Emeritus of Abundance is incredible! At three mana for a 3/4 vigilance, you get to add a high-rate creature to the battlefield nice and early and avoid falling behind. For the low price of two mana (or five if you do it all in one turn), you get a supercharged Eternal Witness . Plus, getting to eight lands to make the Emeritus prepared a second time seems perfectly realistic for green decks in Secrets of Strixhaven Limited. The catch is that, since Regrowth is a sorcery, the Emeritus has to surv

name: Improvisation Capstone
cfbCommentary: .0 Not only is Improvisation Capstone basically unbeatable, but given that there are also so many ways to play this ahead of the curve, it might actually be the best card in the format, all things considered.

name: Stirring Hopesinger
cfbCommentary: .0 This card is ridiculous. There are many ways to enable Repartee, and this should be one of the bigger Limited bombs in the set. Imagine a normal game where you cast a two-drop, three-drop, and then follow it up with Stirring Hopesinger and Dig Site Inventory on turn four. That's basically the game right there.

name: Ral Zarek, Guest Lecturer
cfbCommentary: Ral Zarek, Guest Lecturer neither snowballs nor dominates the game like some of the truly bomb planeswalkers we've seen in past formats. I suspect one of his most common uses will be slowly ticking towards ultimate and creating a sense of urgency for the opponent. Alternatively, you'll sometimes cash him in to return a creature to the battlefield and eat up an opposing attack step. Both of those things are fine, and I'll definitely play Ral when I get him. However, I suspect his value will be cl

name: Antiquities on the Loose
cfbCommentary: Three mana nets you four power and toughness worth of stats, which is already a good deal. Flashback puts this card over the top. Efficient early, game-winning late.

name: Applied Geometry
cfbCommentary: .0 You get a 6/6 with this at worst, since you can target a land if you want to. That's a very high floor, and copying something with a good enter-the-battlefield ability or textbook can be awesome.

name: Informed Inkwright
cfbCommentary: Informed Inkwright is an amazing payoff card for Silverquill decks, but I can also imagine a Jeskai (blue–red-white) build with all the Prismari spells. The common surveil dual lands and cards like Goblin Glasswright or Seize the Spoils should make it pretty easy to play three colors.

name: Magmablood Archaic
cfbCommentary: Magmablood Archaic is, at worst, a four-mana 4/4 with three good abilities in a typical two-color deck, with the potential to be much more than that if you have an easy splash going on or if you are going hard on Converge.

name: Splatter Technique
cfbCommentary: .0 A wrath that turns into a draw four when its services aren't needed is pretty good, and I'd be thrilled to use this technique.

name: Archmage Emeritus
cfbCommentary: .0 Archmage Emeritus is a textbook must-kill card in Limited, and if you untap with it, you almost always win. You can even try to set it up along with a mana-efficient spell in the same turn, locking in some value early on.

name: Murmuring Mystic
cfbCommentary: .0 This is another creature with a target on its back, but unfortunately for your opponent, it also comes with a massive five toughness. Murmuring Mystic is incredible, and you don't even need much to make it worth playing.

name: Traumatic Critique
cfbCommentary: .0 I love when an X-spell is good early, and this functions for as little as two mana (though it won't be killing anything). It can also just burn them out late, and is a fantastic spell to recur in the long game.

name: Vicious Rivalry
cfbCommentary: As long as you have enough life, this is a great sweeper (and does X or less). It also works well if you have the most expensive creature in play, so think of it like a newer version of Toxic Deluge , which is a great card.

name: Wildgrowth Archaic
cfbCommentary: The Archaics are a lot to process because they're weird and confusing, starting right from their mana cost. For example, Wildgrowth Archaic can be cast for GG as a 1/1, or you can spend up to four mana (and up to four different colors) to make it enter bigger. Note that you can choose to spend four mana even if one of the colors is green. And while a big trample, reach creature is nice, the truly great thing about Wildgrowth Archaic is its ability to pump up all of your future creatures. Even in

name: Colorstorm Stallion
cfbCommentary: The Stallion is a massive threat, as one copy leads to two copies the next time, then four, and so forth (well, getting four into play is already enough in most games). The downside is that you usually need to untap with this, so it won't always work out. It's still a great card, and one I'm happy starting with.

name: Vastlands Scavenger // Bind to Life
cfbCommentary: Vastlands Scavenger is yet another incredible prepared creature. Three mana for a 4/4 deathtouch is huge, and "Bind to Life" effectively gives you a whole other threat without investing a second card. The random mill means you can't exactly guarantee what you'll hit, but you should have a great chance to hit a four-drop or larger at instant-speed.

name: Withering Curse
cfbCommentary: .0 I normally rate symmetrical board sweepers at 4.0 or 4.5, but Withering Curse is such an exceptionally strong version of a board sweeper that I'm going to give it a perfect score. For starters, three mana is just so cheap, and gaining life in Secrets of Strixhaven doesn't look difficult at all. But another selling point is that you can craft a game plan around either the unconditional wrath, or the -2/-2 effect. If your opponent overextends, you set it up to reset the board. If you have the b

name: Mind into Matter
cfbCommentary: .0 Mind into Matter may be slow, but it's undeniably powerful. Putting a permanent into play goes a long way towards making sure you are developing your board, though it being tapped can be an issue when you're too low on life. I've liked this card so far, and wouldn't mind starting on this when drafting Quandrix (or splashing it in Witherbloom or Prismari).

name: Blech, Loafing Pest
cfbCommentary: Blech destroyed me in the Early Access event, as it combines incredibly well with all the Pests in the set. This card will do its thing effectively in pretty much every Witherbloom deck, so I wouldn't call it a build-around as much as just a great card.

name: Witherbloom, the Balancer
cfbCommentary: .0 Casting this for five mana isn't too hard, and that's a great deal. Sometimes it'll even be cheaper, and when it is, you might be able to sneak a spell in on the same turn. Not bad, not bad at all, though it will be at its worst when you are losing with an empty board, so keep that in mind.

name: Cauldron of Essence
cfbCommentary: .0 The static on this is already great, and getting to recycle your creatures with enter-the-battlefield abilities (or just bring back high-quality creatures) makes me very interested. Take the Cauldron, fill your deck full of creatures, and profit.

name: Ambitious Augmenter
cfbCommentary: .0 Ambitious Augmenter is really strong. If you cast it on turn 1, it'll start small. But as we know, the tortoise always wins the race eventually. The card will likely be a 3/3 or a 4/4 in no time at all. Your opponent won't be prepared to deal with a creature that large at that point in the game, and even if they can, it'll leave behind a fractal token. Of course, there's some risk of topdecking the Augmenter later in the game, in which case it's a bit weaker, but this is still one of the best

name: Emeritus of Woe // Demonic Tutor
cfbCommentary: .0 Wow! Whoa! Woe! Emeritus of Woe is a total bomb that earns a perfect 5.0 score. A huge body attached to a Demonic Tutor is great value any way you look at it. But now start to imagine that you can find a way to recur the Emeritus, or perhaps a second bomb rare, should you be so lucky. In case that wasn't enough, it's not even hard to make it prepared all over again by trading creatures in combat, or pointing a couple of removal spells at opposing creatures!

name: Erode
cfbCommentary: One mana to destroy any creature at instant speed is incredible efficiency; you just don't want to use Erode too early to give them too much of a mana advantage.

name: Stress Dream
cfbCommentary: Prophetic Bolt is back, though it swapped Impulse for Sleight of Hand . I'm happy either way, as this is the kind of card I have happy dreams about.

name: The Dawning Archaic
cfbCommentary: .0 // 4.0 I'm slamming this in any spell deck — it can end up being completely free! Even at four or five mana, it's still amazing and one attack from this is game over.

name: Additive Evolution
cfbCommentary: Additive Evolution effectively gives you a 4/4 vigilance on the turn you cast it. That means it only takes a few turns before the amount of power and toughness it's distributed outpaces the five mana initial investment. I'm not sure this is quite a bomb, but I did lose badly to it during the MTG Arena Early Access Event. Let's just say you want it on your side of the battlefield and not your opponent's.

name: Grave Researcher // Reanimate
cfbCommentary: I like Grave Researcher just fine, simply due to its ability to surveil every turn. If it sticks around, it's not difficult to set up three creatures in the graveyard and make it prepared. Best of all, you can start preparing it over and over again, turn after turn. For the modest price of three mana, your opponent will have to be very scared of this.

name: Practiced Offense
cfbCommentary: Practiced Offense is basically Dig Site Inventory on steroids. It's a lot more expensive and slower, but it will have an impact later in the game and can likely win you the game.

name: Practiced Scrollsmith
cfbCommentary: The Scrollsmith sometimes does enough simply by exiling a card and triggering all that type of effect, but it's a Snapcaster Mage to boot (and even gives you an extra turn to play the card). I like it, and am happy to take it early.

name: Tragedy Feaster
cfbCommentary: .0 You should play Tragedy Feaster in your black decks, regardless of how much lifegain you have. Even if you have to sacrifice a token or a land, your opponent will just die to the 7/6 trample before the resource disadvantage accumulates too much. The real tragedy is the scenario where you cast Tragedy Feaster , sacrifice something, and then it's answered right away. But as a six-toughness ward creature, that's not necessarily the easiest thing to do.

name: Echocasting Symposium
cfbCommentary: Paradigm spells each look awesome, and Echocasting Symposium is no exception. Sure, six mana for a Clone (and one that can only copy your creatures) is a bit pricey, but this gets you one every turn. Any game where you survive after casting this will cascade quickly in your favor, and I'd windmill slam this pick one and build around it without hesitation. Some good creatures, lots of removal, and this as a finisher — easy.

name: Skycoach Conductor // All Aboard
cfbCommentary: .0 Three keywords on a three-mana 2/3 flier is enough for me, and Skycoach Conductor even comes with a nice little Momentary Blink .

name: Aziza, Mage Tower Captain
cfbCommentary: Aziza is a threatening creature, as she can copy spells for no mana as long as you have a decent board. As such, your opponent will certainly try to answer it, which will usually leave you ahead on mana. You can also try and wait on this until the mid-game, where you play it and a spell in quick succession, ensuring at least one bonus spell.

name: Grim Haruspex
cfbCommentary: .0 Grim Haruspex is black's Special Guests card. It's fragile, but potentially quite powerful as it'll make your opponent unwilling to kill or trade with your other creatures. This is the only creature with Morph in the Limited format. So (at least in theory), you won't be surprising anybody, and you can feel free to simply cast Haruspex face up. I suppose it could come up that you're manascrewed and need to morph it while you wait to find your black mana.

name: Lluwen, Exchange Student // Pest Friend
cfbCommentary: Lluwen, Exchange Student is a veritable Pest factory, which isn't a description most people would relish. Still, this can make a ton of Pests in the average Witherbloom deck, and even waiting until turn six to cast two Pests is a totally reasonable way to play this.

name: Orysa, Tide Choreographer
cfbCommentary: If you are casting Orysa, Tide Choreographer for two mana, you are really doing it, but I'm happy enough to pay full retail. A non-flying Mulldrifter is still a decent deal, and some amount of the time this comes down on the cheap.

name: Poisoner's Apprentice
cfbCommentary: Poisoner's Apprentice isn't quite a three-mana Ravenous Chupacabra because the Infusion ability won't automatically be online every turn if you don't make the effort. That said, this is exactly the type of payoff you want for building around lifegain, so I see this as a premium uncommon. I especially love getting a creature like Poisoner's Apprentice with some ways to recur it from the graveyard.

name: Tam, Observant Sequencer // Deep Sight
cfbCommentary: Tam provides a steady stream of cards and life if it survives, while also being a good play on turn six in order to get instant value. Surely, one of these creatures will live for a turn, and you will then profit wildly.

name: Ark of Hunger
cfbCommentary: I'm hungry for a Big Ark myself, it's a fantastic Product. This card plays into what Lorehold is already doing, while just being an amazing card on its own. It basically draws you an extra card each turn while draining them for one, which is incredible, and sometimes it does even more than that.

name: Colossus of the Blood Age
cfbCommentary: .0 The Colossus is a beating of an uncommon, giving you immediate damage, some amount of lifegain, and a sick trigger when it dies. It always puts you up one card and even lets you cash in useless cards for more action. I've lost to this multiple times, and it always feels impossible to beat.

name: Environmental Scientist
cfbCommentary: In past formats, creatures identical to Environmental Scientist have cost three mana and they've still been very good. Environmental Scientist is a remarkable way to start off a game and guarantee that things are going to go smoothly. This is a subtle but excellent card, and you should be willing to spend a high pick to get it. I appreciate the callback to Environmental Sciences , which was a defining card in original Strixhaven Limited.

name: Exhibition Tidecaller
cfbCommentary: .0 // 3.0 Exhibition Tidecaller being a rare card is a good thing for the format overall, but it does make it less likely this works if you do happen to see it. You aren't getting multiples, and a mill plan based on one card isn't the most robust. If you do have a super spell-heavy deck with enough five-cost cards, this can act as a one-shot threat, as milling 10-20 can legitimately close out the game. I'd be interested in this as a finisher in some specific decks, but mostly would avoid it.

name: Molten-Core Maestro
cfbCommentary: Molten-Core Maestro is basically Expressive Firedancer , but you get to keep the counters, add mana, and it also has menace. It doesn't get much better for a two-mana creature.

name: Scheming Silvertongue // Sign in Blood
cfbCommentary: .0 Two mana for a 1/3 flying, lifelink is already solid, and getting to draw two or four cards off of it makes it a total bomb. Scheming Silvertongue does need a little help to become prepared, but pumping it up or finding just a single point of supplemental lifegain will do the trick.

name: Stirring Honormancer
cfbCommentary: I don't really know what an Honormancer is, but I'll take an extra card (with some nice selection).

name: Flashback
cfbCommentary: .0 If you have a lot of cheap, powerful spells, Flashback might be a reasonable inclusion. Still, it is another card that requires something else and the right timing to actually do something, so don't be too excited about it.

name: Moseo, Vein's New Dean
cfbCommentary: .0 Moseo's Infusion ability isn't the easiest to set up. Still, it's scary enough that opponents will feel a lot of pressure to point a removal spell at the Dean, in which case you've left behind a useful token and gotten a great exchange. Any way you slice it, three mana for two bodies and lots of useful abilities is a great deal, so you should be excited to get Moseo.

name: Prismari, the Inspiration
cfbCommentary: .0 As much as this does inspire me, I have played it, and it's not on the level of other Limited bombs in the set. It costs a ton of mana and doesn't do anything right away, with the Ward often unlikely enough to protect it. If you untap and have some spells, it can go crazy, but that isn't going to happen often enough to make a seven-drop amazing. I'd still play this in Prismari Control, but it's not a windmill slam.

name: Hardened Academic
cfbCommentary: .0 I'm already in for a 2/1 flying haste, as Lorehold definitely can be the beatdown deck in Secrets of Strixhaven Limited. This also gets lifelink when needed, and can distribute some nice counters, making it a strong rare.

name: Harmonized Trio // Brainstorm
cfbCommentary: .0 I'm most definitely in for Brainstorm , and all it takes is a couple of friends to get going. Harmonized Trio doesn't start prepared, but asks very little of you, and a turn or two of this will make the game turn in your favor dramatically. Just make sure you have enough creatures to get this going, at which point it's a one-mana must-kill creature.

name: Essenceknit Scholar
cfbCommentary: .0 Essenceknit Scholar is the perfect Witherbloom card, as it creates a Pest, draws you cards, and is fantastic to get back from the graveyard. There's really no going wrong with this, and you can even play it post-attacks to get a card if a creature died.

name: Forum Necroscribe
cfbCommentary: Forum Necroscribe can be potentially very powerful. However, for six mana, I generally hope for slightly more ability to stabilize the battlefield. Plus, once in a while, you'll manage to stick your powerful six-drop uncommon, and you simply won't have any more gas in your hand to use it. Necroscribe is a solid top-end creature, but perhaps not something you need to cash in with an early pick.

name: Matterbending Mage
cfbCommentary: I'm in without the unblockable ability, and giving Matterbending Mage a little more to do in the mid-to-late game seems fine to me.

name: Vibrant Outburst
cfbCommentary: This is another burn spell that can hit face, and the tap ability works very well on both offense and defense. I'm sold.

name: Foolish Fate
cfbCommentary: I usually give Murder a high grade whenever it appears in a Limited format. So, show me a version that's easier to cast and can frequently hit your opponent for an extra three life, and I'm extremely impressed. I'm all in on Foolish Fate .

name: Impractical Joke
cfbCommentary: .0 The closest we are going to get to a Lightning Bolt in Limited. Super efficient removal.

name: Mana Sculpt
cfbCommentary: This is mostly going to be Cancel , which is mediocre, but every now and then it will get to be Mana Drain (which is not). If you have at least three Wizards and want countermagic, this is a good option. However, it's not a Limited bomb.

name: Pensive Professor
cfbCommentary: .0 If you untap with Pensive Professor , you know you are getting paid, and it's not hard to wait until turn four or five to run this out and get value right away. I didn't have to think much about this one — I'm all over it.

name: Witherbloom Charm
cfbCommentary: .0 The draw mode is great in the late game, and the removal mode is good early, with the lifegain being the most situational. That all adds up nicely, and this is the second-best charm after Silverquill.

name: Fractal Tender
cfbCommentary: Ward 2 helps a lot here, as your main goal is to just untap with this in play. At that point, you will probably be getting a free 3/3, and the game will go swimmingly from there.

name: Garrison Excavator
cfbCommentary: - 4.0 A good payoff for the Lorehold decks, but still requires you to fill your graveyard and find something that consistently exiles the card without having to invest additional resources for it every turn. Garrison Excavator is certainly going to be an excellent card in the right Lorehold deck, but I remain pessimistic about how you will be able to pull things off in time consistently, given how powerful everything else looks in this format.

name: Silverquill Charm
cfbCommentary: This one is much more charming than the rest, offering good removal, a good combat trick, and a bit of damage to close out the game.

name: Topiary Lecturer
cfbCommentary: Topiary Lecturer has felt impressive as a way to generate tons of extra mana over the course of a game. Imagine using this to power out a five-drop on turn four — now your opponent feels a lot of pressure to kill the five-drop, but that leaves behind the Incremented Lecturer to do even more the next turn! If you do manage to play out everything in your hand, then you might find your Lecturer ready to brawl as a 4/5 when all's said and done.

name: Emil, Vastlands Roamer
cfbCommentary: .0 I got to play with Emil in the Magic Arena Early Access Event, and he did some good work in my Quandrix deck, giving trample to fractals and Increment creatures. If you only have, say Forests and Islands, then spending five mana for a 2/2 typically won't be your best use of mana. But you'll often have a dual land, or a splash color. Plus, it's always nice to have mana sink options. Don't be tempted to play an off-color basic land for a single copy of Emil.

name: Killian's Confidence
cfbCommentary: I've been impressed by this so far — perhaps this confidence is earned. This triggers Repartee, which is a solid card-drawing machine, and even nets you value from discard or self-mill when it comes up.

name: Kirol, History Buff // Pack a Punch
cfbCommentary: .0 I mostly like this as a 2/3 for two, but the ability is a real one if the game goes long.

name: Emeritus of Conflict // Lightning Bolt
cfbCommentary: Casting three spells in one turn in a game of Limited is no easy task, but if anyone can do it, it's the Prismari decks in this format, especially thanks to Goblin Glasswright . You also get a 2/2 first strike creature for two mana, which is already a good deal.

name: Grapple with Death
cfbCommentary: A solid removal spell that triggers all your Witherbloom cards? Count me in.

name: Last Gasp
cfbCommentary: .0 Last Gasp has been a top common in previous Limited formats, and I expect no different here. It's efficient and reliable, what's not to like?

name: Lecturing Scornmage
cfbCommentary: I'll be honest that I'm having a tough time evaluating Lecturing Scornmage . For a snowbally one-drop like this, I could see myself being far off in either direction. In the dream scenario, you cast it on turn one and follow up with some combat tricks and removal spells until your opponent finds themselves behind against a 4/4 way before they're prepared to deal with it. On the other hand, you can draw it late when you're gassed out and have a low-impact 1/1. And the big question is: with the ga

name: Paradox Surveyor
cfbCommentary: Even if this almost always gets a land, it's still great, and Quandrix does actually have a decent amount of X-spells in the set.

name: Snooping Page
cfbCommentary: .0 This reads a bit better than it looks, having played with it and against it. It's still quite threatening, but it can be a little slow to get going.

name: Studious First-Year // Rampant Growth
cfbCommentary: .0 Studious First-Year is a great way to start a game of Limited. This is especially true if you're playing a bigger Quandrix or multicolor deck that wants to accelerate into big plays. I've heard some content creators speculate about First-Year being the best green common. While I like a Rampant Growth as much as the next person, I'm slightly cooler on the bear wizard than that. I think I prefer to have Noxious Newt , and I definitely prefer to have the uncommon Environmental Scientist . Here's

name: Suspend Aggression
cfbCommentary: This is a weird card to parse, but I think it plays very similarly to Repulse (which is a great card). They have to replay their card within the one-turn window, which is better than just returning it to hand. Though you also have the same window with your card draw. I like this and will be satisfied with taking it early.

name: Thornfist Striker
cfbCommentary: Thornfist Striker doesn't ask much of you, and it can be a somewhat nasty payoff for all of the little pest tokens you might have running around. I like this as a way to translate gaining life into actually getting the game over with.

name: Wilt in the Heat
cfbCommentary: .0 Even without the discount, this is a decent piece of removal, and in some cases, it'll be very efficient. That's good enough for me.

name: Ennis, Debate Moderator
cfbCommentary: .0 - 3.5 Ennis is Daydream on sticks, enabling you to re-Prepare your creatures or get more value from your other "enter the battlefield" effects. But it is also a payoff card for the "leaves your graveyard" Lorehold mechanic. The card is a strong synergy piece, but not a standalone powerhouse.

name: Proctor's Gaze
cfbCommentary: .0 Board control plus Rampant Growth is a nice little combination, and this does exactly what the Converge deck wants to do. If you are ramping or playing three colors, this is great, and it's solid even in a normal two-color deck.

name: Sanar, Unfinished Genius // Wild Idea
cfbCommentary: .0 Most Prismari decks are going to be happy to run Sanar, as you only need to get a couple of Treasure tokens to be happy with him (and the prepared spell is just a bonus).

name: Teacher's Pest
cfbCommentary: .0 This rating may look high, but Teacher's Pest is basically the perfect Witherbloom card. It makes sure your synergies always work, requires extreme measures to kill, and menace means it gets plenty of free attacks. Don't sleep on this, it's very strong.

name: Charging Strifeknight
cfbCommentary: Charging Strifeknight seems like a card made mostly as a great enabler for the Lorehold synergies, but the efficiency and utility are so good that it should be a premium card in any red deck.

name: Artistic Process
cfbCommentary: .0 Artistic Process is a better version of Archaic's Agony , and you only need to have one color of mana to cast it. Again, you can only afford to play so many expensive cards, but I really like the flexibility here.

name: Muse Seeker
cfbCommentary: Even as just a draw and discard option, Muse Seeker fuels spell decks nicely. Once you start on the Opus track, this just looks great. As long as you have more than ten spells, this is more than worth it.

name: Pestbrood Sloth
cfbCommentary: .0 Pestbrood Sloth is a great way to stabilize the battlefield. As a 4/4 reach, it blocks incredibly well in the mid-game. So if your opponent wants to keep connecting in combat, they may need to remove or trade with the Sloth, at which point you get two more bodies for your troubles.

name: Group Project
cfbCommentary: .0 Decent value two-drop that you will mostly appreciate in Lorehold decks, where the easy flashback cost enables your "leaves your graveyard" synergies without having to invest additional mana.

name: Leech Collector // Bloodletting
cfbCommentary: Leech Collector offers a way to translate repeated lifegain into a non-combat win condition that might be very scary for opponents. However, it doesn't enter prepared, and doesn't self-enable. In other words, I think this will find some nice homes, but I don't see it as a high-pick, premium card.

name: Postmortem Professor
cfbCommentary: Postmortem Professor's stats are modest enough that it won't dominate the game like a true bomb creature can. Still, by threatening a four-point lifeswing each attack, this thing is a real threat, and recurring it multiple times in a game isn't difficult.

name: Scolding Administrator
cfbCommentary: Silverquill likes its administrators, apparently, and this looks like a solid reason to be in that school. It grows quickly and gives you a rebate when it dies, making it a great two-drop.

name: Startled Relic Sloth
cfbCommentary: This gives you a great combatant and triggers all your graveyard stuff — better move fast on the Sloth before it disappears from the pack.

name: Steal the Show
cfbCommentary: This seems like a lot of work to achieve what Unsubtle Mockery does without any hassle. Steal the Show has some extra utility in the late game, but it is awful early. If it could target the opponent, I would be more excited about it, as it could serve as a finisher for the Prismari decks, going as hard on spells as possible. As is, I am not a big fan.

name: Thunderdrum Soloist
cfbCommentary: Between Goblin Glasswright, Hydro-Channeler , Landscape Painter (and also Essence Scatter and Tome Blast ), which are all significantly better, I am not expecting Prismari to be interested in more two-mana creatures.

name: Together as One
cfbCommentary: This is the reason to draft Converge, and a card I'm happy taking first. It is basically Cruel Ultimatum , and ends games in a similar fashion.

name: Cuboid Colony
cfbCommentary: Cuboid Colony gets out of control quickly, and even at 2/2 or 3/3 it's a great deal. It'll usually grow even more than that unless you draw it late, and almost always demands a fast answer.

name: Eternal Student
cfbCommentary: There are two ways to play Eternal Student . First, you can cast it, trade off the 4/2, and make the tokens for full value. Second, you can try to mill or discard it to create tokens on the cheap. Both options are awesome, making this a really desirable card.

name: Quandrix Charm
cfbCommentary: .0 They really went conservative on the Charm cycle, though all of them are fine playables. This is mostly Quench with upside, which I don't mind.

name: Soaring Stoneglider
cfbCommentary: A great card if you have enough ways to fill your graveyard and make this a three mana 4/3 flyer, which are stats way above rate. With so much surveil and rummaging, this should be relatively trivial to do in this set. At worst, you will have to pay the full five, which is still acceptable for a vigilance flyer of this size.

name: Social Snub
cfbCommentary: .0 This card is interesting but weak, and making both players sacrifice means it's really hard to get much of an advantage from it.

name: Burrog Barrage
cfbCommentary: .0 The +1/+0 addition is cute, but in reality, you can evaluate Burrog Barrage similarly to Rocky Rebuke and all of the other variants that have come before it. In all cases, it's quite good and is exactly what green wants in a removal spell.

name: Dissection Practice
cfbCommentary: Dissection Practice is an example of taking a good card ( Skulduggery ) and giving it a small bonus, creating something truly exceptional. Dissection Practice can be a really removal spell with upside, a really nice combat trick, and/or a way to trigger your lifegain synergies cheaply at instant speed. This might go around the draft table on Prerelease weekend, but my prediction is that people will come to value and respect it very highly, very quickly.

name: Encouraging Aviator // Jump
cfbCommentary: The bird is the word, which in this case is "Jump". The Aviator gives you solid stats and the ability to Jump a teammate every turn, which is a nice start to an offense. It's also a good Repartee enabler if you find some cross-color synergies.

name: Molten Note
cfbCommentary: The most important part to understand about Molten Note is that it counts the red and white mana you spend towards the damage dealt, so it's pretty efficient. For three or four mana, you can kill most things, and spending a little more isn't that bad either. The flashback cost is pricey but basically free, as this would be playable without it.

name: Old-Growth Educator
cfbCommentary: .0 Perfectly playable, not particularly exciting — Witherbloom at its finest.

name: Prismari Charm
cfbCommentary: .0 Another medium charm, the Prismari one, mostly shines when you are using the damage mode against multiple small creatures.

name: Spectacular Skywhale
cfbCommentary: .0 Yet another huge threat if it survives, the Skywhale is a decent way to apply pressure. It does start out small, so I'm not over the moon about it, but I'd play it in Prismari most of the time.

name: Stand Up for Yourself
cfbCommentary: Conditional removal spell that can get really awkward if you are staring down something like Informed Inkwright or Sanar, Unfinished Genius . Worth noting is that you can use the plentiful pump spells in this format to increase the power of your opponent's creature and make it big enough for Stand Up for Yourself to deal with it. I don't think this card is actually that much better than Ajani's Response .

name: Unsubtle Mockery
cfbCommentary: Four damage at instant speed is a good deal; the surveil is a nice bonus that you will appreciate mostly in Lorehold, where it can help fuel your graveyard synergies.

name: Wander Off
cfbCommentary: .0 Clean, reliable, instant-speed, and easy to cast. Four mana isn't cheap, but I'm willing to pay for the quality that Wander Off has to offer.

name: Campus Composer // Aqueous Aria
cfbCommentary: You get a lot of stats when Campus Composer gets to play its song — a 3/4 with ward 2 and a 3/3 flier is quite the squad. Ward means this likely lives until you can cast Aqueous Aria, and even if this dies, they probably spent more mana than you did to answer it.

name: Elite Interceptor // Rejoinder
cfbCommentary: .0 Elite Interceptor is basically a Thraben Inspector with some extra utility. Replaces itself, taps a blocker, triggers your Repartee synergies. I quite like this card.

name: Flow State
cfbCommentary: Flow State builds on what you are already doing in most Prismari and Quandrix decks nicely, which is to say, filling them full of sweet spells. Casting this as Anticipate is a fine failure case, and when it works, it's a really nice bit of value. A quick note to say Flow State says instant and sorcery, not either card type. Do be careful when playing this one in Limited, as it might catch you out.

name: Pigment Wrangler // Striking Palette
cfbCommentary: Another amazing value card that enters prepared right away. One mana to copy a spell is a small price to pay, and you also get a 4/4 flyer, which is a huge threat on its own.

name: Quill-Blade Laureate // Twofold Intent
cfbCommentary: Premium two-drop for the Repartee decks that works incredibly well with Graduation Day and Dig Site Inventory . Perfectly fine on its own in any white deck, though.

name: Run Behind
cfbCommentary: .0 Run Behind will play well, as this card slot always does. Four mana anytime or three mana during combat is a good deal, and I'm not cutting any of these.

name: Tome Blast
cfbCommentary: You don't want to have too many Shocks in your deck, but the first copy should be pretty good.

name: Arnyn, Deathbloom Botanist
cfbCommentary: .0 Start with a deathtouch body that can trade with anything. Then give it a passive ability that can be really annoying for the opponent when it sticks around on the battlefield. Now you've got Arnyn, Deathbloom Botanist , who appears to be a very solid uncommon for Limited.

name: Page, Loose Leaf
cfbCommentary: The grandeur part is interesting with Page, Loose Leaf , but ultimately I don't want any of these in my deck, much less two or more.

name: Stadium Tidalmage
cfbCommentary: .0 I'm not really sure why this has a giant tube of toothpaste, or maybe tomato paste, but the stats and ability make it a solid playable.

name: Abstract Paintmage
cfbCommentary: .0 If this survives, you are usually going to be quite happy. That's a big if, but I'd still run this in my Prismari decks. Your opponent doesn't know how good it's going to be, so they likely will prioritize killing it even when it's not great for you.

name: Mind Roots
cfbCommentary: .0 This is a really clever way to do Mind Rot , as it really punishes them for discard lands. Mind Roots is sweet, and a card I'm rarely cutting.

name: Strixhaven Skycoach
cfbCommentary: .0 This is fixing, but it doesn't put that much on the board. If you don't want a 3/2 flier, this isn't all that impressive, so I'd steer clear in Control decks.

name: Blazing Firesinger // Seething Song
cfbCommentary: .0 The vast majority of the cheap creatures that enter prepared seem very strong. Blazing Firesinger works particularly well in Prismari with cards like Visionary's Dance or an X-spell like Traumatic Critique . If you manage to pull off casting Improvisation Capstone with it on turn four, I would honestly feel bad for the opponent.

name: Chelonian Tackle
cfbCommentary: I suspect Chelonian Tackle will play out a little worse than Burrog Barrage , since it's more expensive and sorcery speed. Still, it'll usually get the job done to kill an opposing creature, and you should be happy to play it.

name: Embrace the Paradox
cfbCommentary: .0 I want to give this a higher grade, but there are so many powerful cards once you get into the five mana range. I'm playing this most of the time in Quandrix, but it's not a high priority.

name: Noxious Newt
cfbCommentary: .0 Noxious Newt , on the other hand, is the two-drop that your green decks really want. There are a lot of powerful things to do with extra mana in Secrets of Strixhaven . The deathtouch body allows the Newt to trade with anything, or to power up your "bite" and "fight" spells like Burrog Barrage .

name: Slumbering Trudge
cfbCommentary: .0 Slumbering Trudge looks great. You can spend four mana and get a normal, oversized 6/6 creature. Alternatively, you can invest one or two mana whenever you have it to spare, and then when the plant beast is good and ready, it will wake up and help you dominate the game. I find both of those cases appealing, and the option to choose either one is even better. Even when slumbering, the Trudge powers up your fight and bite spells, or anything that cares about power and toughness on the battlefie

name: Banishing Betrayal
cfbCommentary: .0 While you may be down a card when you use this, being able to create momentum and surveil (at instant speed) is solid interaction, and you won't be cutting Banishing Betrayal often.

name: Bogwater Lumaret
cfbCommentary: .0 Bogwater doesn't sound appealing, and yet this will be a high pick for any Witherbloom deck. It's efficient and gives you zero-mana lifegain, which is all you are really looking for.

name: Dig Site Inventory
cfbCommentary: If you are trying to push damage through, Dig Site Inventory will do a better job than Daydream because the creature can attack right away. As I mentioned above, I believe Dig Site Inventory could be the piece that will tie the white decks together because it fits really well into both Silverquill and Lorehold. My bold prediction is that Dig Site Inventory looks very unassuming, but will be the best white common in the set.

name: End of the Hunt
cfbCommentary: Sometimes, having targeted removal is nice. But honestly, End of the Hunt is so likely to take out the opponent's best threat that I'm not going to be picky. This is similar to Blot Out , which was a constructed-quality removal spell even at three mana, so it's safe to predict that End of the Hunt will be a high pick in draft.

name: Inkshape Demonstrator
cfbCommentary: .0 - 3.25 Inkshape Demonstrator can go from filler to quite good very quickly, depending on how hard your deck is going in on Repartee, especially with some nice ways to buff it.

name: Rubble Rouser
cfbCommentary: Rubble Rouser has bad stats, but might actually be quite valuable because it works as a great enabler in Lorehold, and also does a great job of helping you cast the powerful, expensive Prismari cards ahead of schedule. If I had to make a guess, I would expect this card to start as a bit undervalued, but potentially solidify itself as one of the better red commons.

name: Send in the Pest
cfbCommentary: - 3.0 Send in the Pest looks like Ravenous Rats or any of the hundred other variants we've seen over the years. It loses a tiny bit in not being a creature, since you can't blink it or recur it from the graveyard. On the other hand, the Pest token helps you gain life and fits well into Witherbloom's synergies. I could see cutting this from a very focused, aggressive Silverquill deck with no lifegain synergies. But in most black decks, you'll find a strong reason to include Send in the Pest.

name: Spiritcall Enthusiast // Scrollboost
cfbCommentary: .0 White certainly doesn't seem to have a shortage of three-mana 3/3s. Looking through the set, getting Spiritcall Enthusiast Prepared seems very incidental rather than something you can really build your deck around, but with Trained Armodon as the floor of this card, it is a perfectly fine card to play regardless.

name: Summoned Dromedary
cfbCommentary: .0 Solid grindy value creature that will shine in long games thanks to the recursion. 4/3 stats with vigilance means it attacks and blocks well, giving you a good chance to trade it for something meaningful and getting ahead in the process. Bringing it back also means it "leaves your graveyard", enabling your Lorehold synergies.

name: Elemental Mascot
cfbCommentary: .0 Elemental Mascot has been passable, but isn't really exciting. It takes a lot of effort to get a card out of this, and my Prismari decks have felt more controlling than aggressive thus far. Even in aggressive builds, this doesn't hit that hard, making it a bit out of place in either version.

name: Great Hall of the Biblioplex
cfbCommentary: .0 This is a decent land/creature hybrid, and note that you only need to activate it once, and it stays a creature forever. It's better in spell decks, but reasonable in any deck.

name: Geometer's Arthropod
cfbCommentary: .0 If you have two X-spells, this goes up a little in the ratings, and with three or more, it's a great addition. Most of the time, you are the only person who is interested in this, so I'd try to wheel it. It is strong when it works, and a 1/4 is a decent defensive body.

name: Visionary's Dance
cfbCommentary: .0 Sleight of Hand for two mana on a seven-drop finisher is a great way to hedge your bets, and this is solid in either mode. I like it and would always play it in Prismari.

name: Tester of the Tangential
cfbCommentary: I think a two-mana 1/1 with Increment is already good enough, and Tester of the Tangential kindly shares its counters if the game goes long. What you really want to look for are creatures with evasion, as buffing them goes a long way.

name: Deluge Virtuoso
cfbCommentary: .0 I'm in for Frost Lynx -like card already, and Deluge Virtuoso even has a nice pump ability to boot. The more aggressive you are, the more this will perform, but it's good in any blue deck.

name: Duel Tactics
cfbCommentary: .0 I don't see too many one-toughness creatures in this format, and I am not interested in paying three mana total for a sorcery-speed Shock , so I wouldn't play this card for the damage. It may find some use in a fast Lorehold deck that is playing some powerful Repartee cards, but other than that, I don't really see the appeal.

name: Eager Glyphmage
cfbCommentary: Another filler that can do the job if you really need a creature to fill your four-drop slot, but you won't be excited about it. The card increases in value if you have ways to go wide and buff your entire board with cards such as Practiced Offense .

name: Fractal Mascot
cfbCommentary: There's absolutely nothing wrong with Fractal Mascot , but as a six-drop, I don't think you need to heavily prioritize it. This card does slow down their offense or tap down a blocker, and a 6/6 trample is solid, so I'm happy to play one.

name: Goblin Glasswright // Craft with Pride
cfbCommentary: Goblin Glasswright might look unassuming, but I am confident it will be one of the best commons in the format. It helps you cast all the busted spells ahead of the curve, makes it easy to splash, and helps you easily cast multiple spells in one turn if you have something like Emeritus of Conflict . That's some really good value and utility for a two-drop.

name: Homesickness
cfbCommentary: Expensive spells are looking pretty good with Opus and Increment as themes, and Homesickness is a strong card by itself. I'm always homesick for more card draw, and this keeps you alive while fueling you back up (much like a care package from home).

name: Pest Mascot
cfbCommentary: .0 I've seen Pest Mascot grow pretty big a few times, but it's also a great removal or tempo target once you've put the effort in. Witherbloom decks will play this happily, but won't be taking it too early.

name: Pterafractyl
cfbCommentary: .0 Card Name: 5.0 Easily the best name in the set, Pterafractyl is also a good Quandrix card. It scales nicely, gains a little life, and is an X-spell for the cards that care about that.

name: Pursue the Past
cfbCommentary: .0 This is totally worth pursuing for most Lorehold decks, but it isn't a really high priority. You have a lot of ways to spend mana, and this isn't doing anything too special.

name: Decorum Dissertation
cfbCommentary: .0 I just told you that drawing two cards and losing two life is an unexciting effect. Well, if you can do it every turn for the rest of the game for no additional mana investment, that certainly changes things! Something that I love about Paradigm is that you can always decline to cast the copy, so if you've drawn six extra cards and feel like that's enough (or if you start to worry about decking or bleeding out), then you can take a break. Decorum Dissertation isn't a perfect 5.0 because somet

name: Restoration Seminar
cfbCommentary: Restoration Seminar is quite expensive, but it should comfortably win you any late game. Get ready for Paradigm to challenge your strategic decision-making by carefully selecting the best creature to bring back from your graveyard every turn for the rest of the game. If you first pick a card like Restoration Seminar , you will want to focus on building a slower and grindy deck that will have inevitability thanks to it. With so much surveil in the format and cards like Grave Researcher and Forum

name: Hydro-Channeler
cfbCommentary: .0 In the spell-heavy deck, Hydro-Channeler is a great way to get ahead of the curve (and it even blocks decently well). As long as you have ten or more spells, this is likely worth playing.

name: Lorehold Charm
cfbCommentary: .0 I'm not cutting this from my Lorehold decks, mainly because the pump effect is strong, but it's not a very exciting member of the Charm cycle.

name: Codie, Vociferous Codex
cfbCommentary: .0 // 4.0 In the all-spells deck (and I mean just about entirely non-permanents in this case), Codie is awesome. Adding a mana and cascading is a fantastic ability, even if the deckbuilding cost is high. I'd definitely try to build around this if I saw it early.

name: Dualcaster Mage
cfbCommentary: .0 Dualcaster Mage is more expensive than Choreographed Sparks , but can copy any instant or sorcery spell on the stack, including your opponent's. That makes it a much better card, as you can usually find a good timing to make it an easy two-for-one by copying their removal or card advantage spell.

name: Oracle's Restoration
cfbCommentary: Oracle's Restoration is a cheap way to turn on Infusion. (Or Repartee if you somehow have a bunch of Silverquill creatures in your green deck). But in the end, this is such a mopey, low-impact card that it's hard to imagine ever being terribly excited about it. Once again, it's not a disaster if it ends up in your deck, but it's not something to spend picks on.

name: Potioner's Trove
cfbCommentary: I've liked the Trove a fair amount, as it enables Converge decks and gives spell decks a bit of lifegain for the long game.

name: Tablet of Discovery
cfbCommentary: Red truly has no shortage of creating extra mana in this format. Tablet of Discovery is yet another card that allows you to play the busted X-spells and cards like Stress Dream , Visionary's Dance , or a splashed Embrace the Paradox or Mind into Matter ahead of the curve. In addition to that, if you draw it in the late game, which is usually when mana rocks are at their worst, you can even get a free card from it. I like this card a lot.

name: Rapturous Moment
cfbCommentary: .0 I've been impressed with Rapturous Moment , having had it in three decks during early access. It triggers your Opus cards, lets you multi-spell easily, and provides a lot of card filtering for just one mana net. It also goes crazy with the Lute, as you tap three lands and are left with five mana that can cast creatures.

name: Strife Scholar // Awaken the Ages
cfbCommentary: This card looks like good value at first sight, but the body is quite mediocre as it will always trade down for a two-drop, and paying six mana for Awaken the Ages is quite a lot. I expect this card to be a lot worse than it looks.

name: Zealous Lorecaster
cfbCommentary: Zealous Lorecaster is a bit too expensive with too small a body for the mana invested. It looks like a decent card, and lower-power formats it probably would be, but this set seems like it just simply has too many good cards for something like this to be good enough. Red seems really strong to me for many reasons. There are a lot of good value cards, ways to get ahead on cards and other resources, and even multiple cards that rummage to smooth out your draws. The multicolored cards are incredibl

name: Expressive Firedancer
cfbCommentary: .0 Fine filler for the Prismari decks, though I suspect you will usually rather go for Goblin Glasswright , Hydro-Channeler , or Essence Scatter to fill the two-mana slot.

name: Infirmary Healer // Stream of Life
cfbCommentary: Stream of Life is rarely going to be an exciting way to spend your mana. But since it comes attached to a perfectly reasonable body, Infirmity Healer offers a low-cost way to enable Infusion, Opus, and the various Quandrix cards that reference "X spells".

name: Inkling Mascot
cfbCommentary: .0 In the dedicated Repartee deck, I'd play this, but I'm not jumping for joy about it.

name: Landscape Painter // Vibrant Idea
cfbCommentary: .0 A 2/1 that lets you cash in a draw two later? I'm sold, even if the draw part is on the expensive side. My main advice here is not to get overly attached to drawing two, and if you can trade off Landscape Painter early, I would consider it a happy little accident.

name: Melancholic Poet
cfbCommentary: Melancholic Poet is a decent two-drop that gives you a little extra, and synergizes well with the themes of both Silverquill and Witherbloom. That's about the definition of a solid filler card.

name: Mindful Biomancer
cfbCommentary: Mindful Biomancer is a really nice two-drop. If you trade it off, then the point of lifegain is a nice bonus. And due to the threat of its activated ability, this is actually quite annoying to block.

name: Quick Study
cfbCommentary: .0 Divination has been powercrept out for a while now, so I'm just happy to get an instant-speed version of it in Quick Study .

name: Rehearsed Debater
cfbCommentary: Another good filler with solid stats and a useful ability.

name: Shattered Acolyte
cfbCommentary: There are 16 cards with the artifact or enchantment type in this set, but most of them immediately create some value, and only about a handful of them seem worth destroying. That makes Shattered Acolyte a fine filler card that goes up in value the more ways you have to buff your creatures, like with Dig Site Inventory .

name: Stone Docent
cfbCommentary: Fine two-drop that looks like an important piece of the puzzle for the Lorehold decks, where you want to fill your graveyard naturally as the game goes, with cards you can exile or flashback instead of having to spend too much time on doing that with cards that do not affect the board.

name: Tackle Artist
cfbCommentary: Another fine filler that will mostly fit in Prismari.

name: Snarl Song
cfbCommentary: - 4.0 Snarl Song is weak for a normal two-color deck. But it scales up to be incredibly strong if you can spend four or five different colors of mana on it. This makes it an ideal payoff for a multicolor converge deck, but many green drafters at the table won't want it.

name: Lumaret's Favor
cfbCommentary: Lumaret's Favor is a very strong version of a category of cards (combat tricks) your green decks won't particularly need. It's possible to use this to win two combat exchanges at the same time, but the setup required for that is pretty elaborate. This is another case where you can put Lumaret's Favor in your Witherbloom decks when you have it, but likely shouldn't be picking it terribly highly.

name: Shopkeeper's Bane
cfbCommentary: I typically dislike 4/2s for three mana, since they're very likely to trade down against two-drop creatures. Shopkeeper's Bane makes up for that with trample and an attack trigger, meaning that even when it trades down on mana, you likely get a reasonable life-total swing along with it. This looks like a middle-of-the-road filler creature.

name: Spellbook Seeker // Careful Study
cfbCommentary: .0 I'd say a Careful Study is more than enough to get me to play a 3/3 flier for four, and I anticipate Spellbook Seeker being a staple common in the format.

name: Cheerful Osteomancer // Raise Dead
cfbCommentary: .0 Whenever possible, you'll want to cast Cheerful Osteomancer's Raise Dead spell right away, before giving the opponent a chance to remove the fragile body. In other words, this feels a lot like five mana for a 4/2 Gravedigger , which is a late-game play I'm definitely in for.

name: Daydream
cfbCommentary: .0 - 2.5 An interesting utility card that needs the right home with a lot of creatures with good "enter the battlefield" effects like Poisoner's Apprentice , Matterbending Mage , or Orysa, Tide Choreographer . The most obvious use of Daydream should be in combination with creatures that enter Prepared, which means you can basically "reset" them that way and use them again. Flicker -type cards are also good in a format with many cards like Pacifism or Sleep , but I don't see those cards in this s

name: Essence Scatter
cfbCommentary: .0 This style of spells always plays well in Limited, and I see no reason why Secrets of Strixhaven would be different. At just two mana, Essence Scatter is affordable, effective, and a good stand-in for a two-drop (especially on the draw, where you get to nab their three-mana play).

name: Primary Research
cfbCommentary: .0 - 4.0 Five mana is generally too much to bring back something that costs three or less, unless it is a bomb like Stirring Hopesinger . The more exciting part is using this as a value engine by drawing extra cards, but to consistently have something leave your graveyard every turn does not seem trivial. It pairs incredibly well with Rubble Rouser , which even enables you to get it into play on turn four. Ascendant Dustspeaker is another card that allows you to exile a card every turn, but gett

name: Sundering Archaic
cfbCommentary: .0 I love this as a removal option in Converge, and the activated ability comes up a lot. It can mess with their graveyard stuff or prevent you from being decked, both of which are relevant.

name: Brush Off
cfbCommentary: I'd be happy to mainboard Brush Off in Sealed, and would consider it in Draft if you have a lot of other instant-speed options. When you leave four mana up, and they don't play anything, you want to be able to spend that mana on something.

name: Growth Curve
cfbCommentary: .0 // 3.0 I would avoid playing a sorcery speed pump spell most of the time, but if you are playing the dedicated Fractal deck, then this likely is worth it. Once it's giving you around five counters, it's a real card, and combine this with some trample for some nice game-ending potential.

name: Heated Argument
cfbCommentary: .0 That's the third red five-mana burn spell, which means these will be very easy to come by. At least Heated Argument is an instant, which makes it playable, though still not too exciting.

name: Interjection
cfbCommentary: .0 If I am low on combat tricks, I don't mind a copy of Interjection in my deck. Not a card I am usually looking to play, but a perfectly serviceable trick that gets a little bit more value in Repartee decks.

name: Living History
cfbCommentary: Good filler for the Lorehold decks, but that's about it.

name: Sneering Shadewriter
cfbCommentary: In Lorwyn Eclipsed earlier this year, Dawnhand Eulogist showed up as a surprisingly high-performer (actually, the best-performing common by many stats). These creatures that drain life and have decent bodies do a lot of work because often your opponent has to remove or trade with them, at which point they've left behind a lot of value and improved your position in the game. Sneering Shadewriter could end up being similar.

name: Ascendant Dustspeaker
cfbCommentary: A classic filler that you include if you need a curve-topper, but nothing to write home about. The card goes up in value if you are in Lorehold and need something that repeatedly exiles cards from your graveyard to enable your synergies.

name: Imperious Inkmage
cfbCommentary: The Inkmage isn't quite strong enough to really justify being imperious, but who really does have a good sense of their own self-importance? This is a fine playable, but nothing worth taking too early.

name: Planar Engineering
cfbCommentary: - 3.0 Some of your big Quandrix or multicolor decks will be interested in going up two lands for the price of four mana. There's also a non-negligible effect of deck thinning if you play a long game after removing four lands from your library. (Emphasizing that you should consider this for long games — deck thinning over the course of a couple of draw steps is not particularly useful). Don't play Planar Engineering in any old deck. On turn four, most decks want to be adding to the battlefield ra

name: Fractalize
cfbCommentary: I'm intrigued by Fractalize — it's a combination pump spell and shrink spell all in one. By casting it with X=0, you can make their creature into a 1/1, and by casting it for at least three mana, you can grow your own creature. That's an interesting bit of flexibility, and while neither side is amazing, the combination seems worth trying.

name: Rabid Attack
cfbCommentary: You can dream about setting up ridiculous turns with Rabid Attack , but I think the median effect of casting this really won't be that impressive. In a lot of cases, its effect will be roughly as good as Masterful Flourish at twice the mana and a higher rarity, so I'm not exactly foaming at the mouth over this one.

name: Tenured Concocter
cfbCommentary: I can't say that I care much about the Infusion ability of Tenured Concocter . However, the ward ability to draw a card when the opponent points a removal spell at this makes it a very solid defensive five-drop for stabilizing the battlefield. As always, common five-drop creatures aren't your top priority, but this is a totally solid one that can make the cut in a lot of your green decks.

name: Diary of Dreams
cfbCommentary: .0 // 3.5 In the Prismari all-spells deck, this has been quite strong for me. Once you've cast a couple of spells, it snowballs quickly, and a free card each turn ends the game easily. It isn't great in other decks, so I'd consider it a build-around.

name: Follow the Lumarets
cfbCommentary: Since most Limited decks are primarily composed of creatures and lands, you'll almost always be able to pluck two cards off of an infused Follow the Lumarets . Two cards for two mana is great, and even getting one card (while tempo negative) isn't a disaster. You'll be happy to include a copy or two of Follow the Lumarets in decks that are good at gaining life.

name: Graduation Day
cfbCommentary: .0 - 3.5 Graduation Day is a nice, cheap payoff for a dedicated Repartee deck. Between Elite Interceptor , Dig Site Inventory , and some powerful rares like Informed Inkwright , I can definitely imagine some low-to-the-ground white decks taking full advantage of this mechanic.

name: Magus of the Library
cfbCommentary: .0 Magus of the Library is the first of green's two Special Guest cards. I like it, since a two-drop mana dork is serviceable, and every once in a while, you can start dominating games with the card-drawing ability. Note that the double-green manacost is not trivial. My 3.0 rating assumes ten lands that tap for green mana in your deck. So if you have nine or eight, you should bump down the rating a little. Below eight and you shouldn't play it.

name: Rapier Wit
cfbCommentary: Good filler that replaces itself, triggers your Repartee synergies, and can sometimes play a good role as a removal spell would in very fast decks if you can close out the game quickly enough afterwards. I like this card.

name: Efflorescence
cfbCommentary: In a high-power format like Secrets of Strixhaven , you often just don't find room for combat tricks like Efflorescence . If it winds up in your deck, it's not a disaster, and it will do some good work once in a while, but I really don't envision myself picking this over solid creatures, removal, card advantage, or mana ramp.

name: Render Speechless
cfbCommentary: More like Render Sideboard, as that's usually where this is going to live. Even with Repartee, this is too expensive to be a mainstay.

name: Mage Tower Referee
cfbCommentary: If you need a two-drop, you need a two-drop.

name: Root Manipulation
cfbCommentary: If you have at least three creatures out, Root Manipulation will likely be strong, but therein lies the rub. It's not always going to work out that way, and some of the times cards like this are just dead draws. I'd play it as a finisher, but not every deck needs that.

name: Scathing Shadelock // Venomous Words
cfbCommentary: Scathing Shadelock offers good stats, plus a repeatable way to unload damage and trigger Repartee. That sounds like a good version of a five-drop creature, though it's good to remember that five-drop creatures generally aren't the very top priority in booster draft.

name: Transcendent Archaic
cfbCommentary: .0 Like Sundering Archaic , this does great work when you enable it. It gives you a giant monster and a grip of cards, so you will win most games where you survive the turn.

name: Choreographed Sparks
cfbCommentary: .0 - 2.0 Normally, I am not a huge fan of cards that copy something on the stack because you need another good card to make them relevant, not to mention the right timing to cast them. That said, there are so many good spells in this format that it should be fairly trivial to find something like Seize the Spoils (keep in mind that you don't have to discard another card) or Unsubtle Mockery to copy with it. Still, it's usually better to have a decent card that works on its own instead of going th

name: Cost of Brilliance
cfbCommentary: // 2.5 Three mana and two life for two cards just isn't a great deal these days. So you'll want to keep Cost of Brilliance just to your Silverquill decks where you're trying to trigger Repartee.

name: Pull from the Grave
cfbCommentary: I'm a big fan of graveyard recursion, and Pull from the Grave is clearly a strong version of this style of card. That said, one of the first things I look at in a new Limited format is how easy it is to recur creatures from the graveyard, and Secrets of Strixhaven has no shortage of options. That leaves Pull from the Grave as a good effect, but one that you might not necessarily need to prioritize early in a draft.

name: Rearing Embermare
cfbCommentary: Classic average filler if you need a curve-topper.

name: Seize the Spoils
cfbCommentary: Great filler that you never pick up too early, but never mind having it in your deck as it always helps smooth out your draws, while also ramping and fixing your mana for a potentially splashed third color. Given how good Prismari looks, I have a feeling this might be the most common card you will be seeing in Luis-Scott Vargas' Limited videos.

name: Spirit Mascot
cfbCommentary: .0 One counter already makes this good, and multiple counters really get the school spirit going. Lorehold decks will basically always want this, though they don't need to take it early.

name: Masterful Flourish
cfbCommentary: Masterful Flourish is good for triggering Repartee, and matches up nicely against certain "destroy" or damage-based removal spells. Outside of that, it's a little underwhelming, so seek to only play it in the right deck.

name: Ulna Alley Shopkeep
cfbCommentary: .0 Ulna Alley Shopkeep can hit hard, but it doesn't do anything outside of combat, and isn't even particularly great as a blocker. A clear example of a filler card.

name: Mathemagics
cfbCommentary: ^1 As funny as this card is, I think we can probably put some actual numbers on it and see how Mathemagics does. For two mana, it draws one, and for four mana, it draws two — both of those options are quite weak. For six mana, it draws four cards (which is now worth thinking about), and at eight to even ten mana, you start to consider pointing the spell at your opponent. The way I see this, Mathemagics is a mediocre card draw spell that can also be an expensive win condition, which is something

name: Muse's Encouragement
cfbCommentary: .0 I like what you get with Muse's Encouragement , even if it is pricey. Five mana for an instant-speed 3/3 and surveil 2 is perfect for the spell-based decks, and there are plenty of nice little synergies in this set.

name: Nita, Forum Conciliator
cfbCommentary: Nita usually will require an untap to work, but work she does. You get to trade a creature for a spell and a bunch of +1/+1 counters, which is a fantastic deal. If they can't kill this, you often will win the game as long as you have a couple of creatures in play.

name: Owlin Historian
cfbCommentary: Fine filler that enables your Lorehold synergies while also benefiting from them.

name: Wild Hypothesis
cfbCommentary: I can't say I'm the biggest fan of Wild Hypothesis . Even factoring in the scalability and the surveil 2, you're always getting an under-rate creature. And it's even a token that can get blown out by bounce and blink cards. The best time to play Wild Hypothesis is if you're trying to trigger Opus or get some additional "X spells" into your deck.

name: Zimone's Experiment
cfbCommentary: Zimone's Experiment is a fun card that can give you mana ramping, or put action cards into your hand, or do a mix of both things. The important thing to note is that you need to be at least somewhat interested in the mana ramping, or else the Experiment will be too slow for many games. That said, in a "go-big" deck, I love the idea that you can put two lands onto the battlefield when your hand is heavy with action, or you can find a big creature if you already have the mana and you need somethin

name: Mica, Reader of Ruins
cfbCommentary: .0 - 4.0 There are only three artifacts in the entire format that cost less than three mana, so you cannot reliably draft an artifact deck. That said, Goblin Glasswright and Seize the Spoils both make an artifact treasure token, and it also works nicely with Tablet of Discovery , so there are some nice synergies you can put together without much effort. A four-mana 4/4 with ward is also not embarrassing.

name: Hungry Graffalon
cfbCommentary: .0 Just some stats on a ground creature. Hungry Graffalon is a totally fine card to put in your deck, but I'd generally hope to do better at the four-mana slot. Don't spend high picks on it.

name: Chase Inspiration
cfbCommentary: This type of trick always plays better than it looks, and saving your creature from pretty much any fate is certainly worth one mana. The better the creatures in your deck, the better Chase Inspiration becomes, and it's not hard to set up a turn where you can protect one of your important cards.

name: Divergent Equation
cfbCommentary: .0 // 3.0 In a normal deck, Divergent Equation is likely too slow and/or situational, but in Prismari packed full of spells, I'm all over it. Casting this for seven mana on a stable board is the dream, as you can get back two removal spells and a card draw spell (or better yet, two card draw spells, maybe three?). The trick is getting to that stable board first, which this card does not help with at all. It's also neat in a Quandrix deck as it's an expensive card for Increment, though I expect t

name: Rancorous Archaic
cfbCommentary: .0 This is a solid playable in the Converge deck, but it isn't a reason to go into the deck. As with most converge cards, you need three colors to make them decent, and four to five colors to make them good.

name: Textbook Tabulator
cfbCommentary: .0 Textbook Tabulator does set up your draws nicely, but also starts a bit weak. I'd play this if you are short on three-drops, but don't think it's worth going out of your way to snap one up.

name: Adrix and Nev, Twincasters
cfbCommentary: Most Quandrix or Witherbloom decks will be pretty good at making tokens, so I don't mind speculating on Adrix and Nev — it's hard to kill, and just one extra token makes it worth it.

name: Wisdom of Ages
cfbCommentary: .0 // 3.0 Wisdom of Ages is basically the same thing as Divergent Equation at the high end, but you can't cast it for cheaper. That's a lot worse, though I'd still try to build around this if I had the opportunity.

name: Borrowed Knowledge
cfbCommentary: .0 Here, borrow some knowledge from me — this card is abysmal, and you should never play it. You want to cast card draw spells in the late game, and by that point, this does basically nothing.

name: Comforting Counsel
cfbCommentary: .0 - 2.5 Comforting Counsel is an interesting and aspirational rare. Since it's (a) not similar to any card that's come before it, and (b) so dependent on the context of the format, it's very tricky to evaluate. What I can say is that enchantments which have no immediate impact and require this level of set-up are often duds. Take comfort in the knowledge that you absolutely will not want this in an average green deck. If you're a highly-focused Witherbloom deck with tons of repeatable lifegain,

name: Fractal Anomaly
cfbCommentary: .0 If you can draw at least three cards with this, then Fractal Anomaly becomes a good deal. This card is the perfect way to make sure you're getting on the board while you spend time drawing cards. I dream of having a deck that can use this well, and note that this does count your draw step as long as you play it during your turn. If you have three or four big card draw spells and a couple of cantrips, I'd experiment with the Anomaly.

name: Fix What's Broken
cfbCommentary: .0 Unfortunately, they fixed what was broken by making this return only cards that cost X, not X or less. That means it usually won't do enough to justify paying a bunch of life, which is disappointing.

name: Glorious Decay
cfbCommentary: // 2.5 Glorious Decay has enough flexibility that it'll be perfectly fine if it winds up as your 23rd mainboard card. That said, the bulk of the value is having it as a sideboard card for best-of-three play. In other words, you can mostly ignore it if you're drafting on the MTG Arena ladder. Otherwise, look for opportunities to grab it on the cheap so you have more ways to deal with bomb enchantments, artifacts, and flying creatures after sideboarding.

name: Dina's Guidance
cfbCommentary: .0 I don't want to spend three mana looking for a creature, even if I have the option of putting it in my graveyard. Save this one for Constructed.

name: Library of Leng
cfbCommentary: .0 This is not close to being worth a card — do not put it in your deck. This only appears in paper, but it's irrelevant, since it won't be appearing in any decks.

name: Arcane Omens
cfbCommentary: Arcane Omens can be very powerful when you successfully empty your opponent's hand of three, four, or five cards. That said, there are also fail cases. Your opponent might go empty-handed before you cast it. Or you might fall behind on the battlefield and be unable to cast it at all. There will be situations and matchups where Omens feels like the best card ever. But when you combine the inherent volatility with the deckbuilding costs, this really isn't the Converge payoff that I'm looking for.

name: Harsh Annotation
cfbCommentary: .0 Just like Crib Swap , this card is mostly a trap. These days, most creatures immediately create some value, so to use removal on them that gives your opponent even more value is rarely a good answer. I'd usually only run this type of card if I am desperate for removal or bring it out of my sideboard if my opponent shows me some unbeatable haymakers in Stirring Hopesinger or Prismari, the Inspiration . Why is Erode much better? Mostly because one and two mana is a huge difference. There are us

name: Procrastinate
cfbCommentary: .0 I love both procrastinating and Procrastinate , as this effectively removes a creature for as long as you want. The key is that it gives you twice X, so for three or four mana, you can usually lock down a creature until the game just about ends. It's also an X-spell, which scales nicely with a number of mechanics in the set.

name: Zaffai and the Tempests
cfbCommentary: .0 // 3.5 How good this is depends entirely on what spells you have to play with this. With some nice six or seven-mana haymakers, it's great, and without that, it's likely not worth the cost. I've liked it so far, but you need to make sure it's supported.

name: Pox Plague
cfbCommentary: .0 At the cost of a whopping five black mana, Pox Plague is virtually impossible for most decks to cast. Even when you can resolve it, it's unclear if the volatile symmetrical effect will wind up being good for you. This one is just for fun, and you shouldn't pick it if your goal is to win games.

name: Resonating Lute
cfbCommentary: .0 This is my favorite card in the set, and I think it's legit incredible. If you are a spell-heavy deck, this basically doubles your mana, and can even start drawing cards once you start rolling out the card draw. I've had this be awesome for me , and am going to be taking this first pick every time I see it.
